summary: Gruber argues that Apple Music's confusing interface stems from trying to combine streaming and owned music into one app, and that a standalone streaming app would be the clearest solution. He draws a parallel between iOS's philosophy of small, focused apps and the bloated monolithic approach of desktop iTunes. He frames the upcoming WWDC revamp as a two-part test: whether Apple can simplify the product design, and whether they can present it coherently. He contends that product design coherence and marketing coherence are fundamentally linked, citing last year's disastrous Apple Music keynote segment as proof.
key_points: Apple Music should be a standalone app because combining streaming and owned music creates inherent UI confusion
iOS succeeded by favoring small focused apps over monolithic ones, but Apple Music violates this principle
Last year's Apple Music WWDC keynote was the worst segment of a modern Apple keynote, reflecting the product's incoherence
Coherence in product design leads to coherence in marketing — and incoherence breeds incoherence
Despite UI problems, Apple Music is growing well with 13 million paying subscribers in nine months
money_quotes: "All your music in one app" sounds like a great idea in theory, but in practice, I believe that is what has led to the confusing UI.
"Everything you see is in the cloud, and you have access to it because you are a subscriber" is easy to understand. "Some of this is in the cloud, some of this you own" is more complicated.
A big aspect of iOS's success, from day one in June 2007, is that it emphasized smaller focused apps that do less over larger monolithic apps that do more.
The bottom line is that a music app shouldn't be confusing.
I don't break out my "This wouldn't have happened if Steve Jobs were still around" stamp very often, but I broke it out for that one.
Coherence in product design leads to coherence in product marketing. And vice versa: incoherence in product design leads to incoherence in product marketing.
If the product isn't logical and consistent throughout, how can it be marketed in a logical and consistent way?
key_insight: A product that can't be explained simply can't be designed simply, and Apple Music's confused identity as both a streaming service and a music library manager undermines both its usability and its marketing.
